title: Witch Week - Chapter 12 (Fragment 2)
text:
Estelle nodded. “Some spell!” Brian said jeeringly.

Brian was plainly determined not to help in any way. He stayed scornfully silent while the rest of them explained the events which had led up to their being here. Nobody told Chrestomanci quite everything. Brian’s contemptuous look made it all feel like a pack of lies anyway. Nan did not mention her meeting with Mr. Wentworth on his hearthrug. She felt rather noble not saying anything about that, considering the way Brian was behaving. She did not mention the way she had described the food, either, though Charles did. On the other hand, Charles did not feel the need to mention the Simon Says spell. Nirupam told Chrestomanci about that, but he somehow forgot to say that Dan Smith had eaten Charles’s shoes. And when the rest of them had finished, Chrestomanci looked at Brian.

“Your narrative now, please,” he said politely.

It was a very powerful politeness. Everyone had thought that Brian was not going to tell anything at all, but, grudgingly, he did. First he admitted to causing the birds in music. Then he claimed that Charles had advised him in the night to run away from school and confuse his trail by blaming the witch. And, while Charles was still stuttering with anger over that, Brian coolly explained that he had discovered Charles was a witch the next morning anyway and got Charles to take him to the matron so that the matron could see the effects of the Evil Eye at first hand. Finally, more grudgingly still, he confessed that he had written the anonymous note to Mr. Crossley and started everything. Then, as an afterthought, he turned on Nan.

“And you kept stealing my broomstick, didn’t you?”

“It’s not yours. It belongs to the school,” said Nan.

At the same time, Charles was saying angrily to Chrestomanci, “It’s not true I advised him to blame the witch!”

Chrestomanci was staring vaguely up into the beech trees and did not seem to hear. “The situation is quite impossible,” he remarked. “Let us all go and see the old lady who used to run the witches’ rescue service.”

This struck them all as an excellent idea. It was clear the old lady could rescue them if she wanted. They agreed vigorously. Nirupam said, “But the police—”

“Invisibly, of course,” said Chrestomanci. He was still obviously thinking of something else. He turned to walk away between the tree trunks, and, as he did so, everyone flicked out of sight. All that could be seen was the rustling circle of autumn beeches. “Come along,” said Chrestomanci’s voice.

There followed a minute or so of almost indescribable confusion. It started with Nan assuming she had no body and walking into a tree. She was just as solid as ever, and knocked herself quite silly for a second. “Oh, sorry!” she said to the tree. The rest of them somehow forced their way under the low branches and found themselves out in the field. There, the first thing everybody saw was two cars parked almost in the hedge below, and a number of people from the cars leaning over the hedge to talk to two policemen. From the way all the people kept pointing up at the woods, it was clear they were describing how they saw two witches ride across the road on a mop and a hoe. That panicked everyone. They all set off the other way, towards the town, in a hurry. But as soon as they did, they saw that there was no one ahead of them and waited for the rest to catch up. Then they heard someone speak some way ahead, and ran to catch up. But of course they could not tell where the people they were running after
were. Shortly, nobody knew where anybody was or what to do about it.

vocabulary.definition: To confess or acknowledge something reluctantly or hesitantly.
vocabulary.example: First he admitted to causing the birds in music.

vocabulary.definition: Offered suggestions about the best course of action to someone.
vocabulary.example: Then he claimed that Charles had advised him in the night to run away from school and confuse his trail by blaming the witch.

vocabulary.definition: Not identified by name; of unknown name.
vocabulary.example: Finally, more grudgingly still, he confessed that he had written the anonymous note to Mr.

vocabulary.definition: To admit or acknowledge something, typically something wrong or embarrassing.
vocabulary.example: Finally, more grudgingly still, he confessed that he had written the anonymous note to Mr.

vocabulary.definition: To make someone unable to think clearly or understand.
vocabulary.example: Then he claimed that Charles had advised him in the night to run away from school and confuse his trail by blaming the witch.

vocabulary.definition: Showing or expressing contempt or disdain.
vocabulary.example: Brian’s contemptuous look made it all feel like a pack of lies anyway.

vocabulary.definition: Having made a firm decision and being resolved not to change it.
vocabulary.example: Brian was plainly determined not to help in any way.

vocabulary.definition: To move something out suddenly or quickly.
vocabulary.example: He turned to walk away between the tree trunks, and, as he did so, everyone flicked out of sight.

vocabulary.definition: In a reluctant or unwilling manner.
vocabulary.example: Everyone had thought that Brian was not going to tell anything at all, but, grudgingly, he did.

vocabulary.definition: A state of urgency causing rapid movement or quick action.
vocabulary.example: They all set off the other way, towards the town, in a hurry.

vocabulary.definition: Not able to occur, exist, or be done.
vocabulary.example: “The situation is quite impossible,” he remarked.

vocabulary.definition: Too unusual, extreme, or indefinite to be adequately described.
vocabulary.example: There followed a minute or so of almost indescribable confusion.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that cannot be seen.
vocabulary.example:
Nirupam said, “But the police—”

“Invisibly, of course,” said Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: In a mocking or derisive manner.
vocabulary.example: ” Brian said jeeringly.

vocabulary.definition: Having high moral qualities or ideals.
vocabulary.example: She felt rather noble not saying anything about that, considering the way Brian was behaving.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing good manners or respect for others.
vocabulary.example: “Your narrative now, please,” he said politely.

vocabulary.definition: Having great power or strength.
vocabulary.example: It was a very powerful politeness.

vocabulary.definition: To save someone from a dangerous or difficult situation.
vocabulary.example: “Let us all go and see the old lady who used to run the witches’ rescue service.

vocabulary.definition: A group or area characterized by a soft, fluttering sound caused by movement.
vocabulary.example: All that could be seen was the rustling circle of autumn beeches.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner expressing contempt or disdain.
vocabulary.example: He stayed scornfully silent while the rest of them explained the events which had led up to their being here.

vocabulary.definition: Looking fixedly or vacantly at someone or something with wide eyes.
vocabulary.example:
”

Chrestomanci was staring vaguely up into the beech trees and did not seem to hear.

vocabulary.definition: Taking something without permission or legal right.
vocabulary.example: “And you kept stealing my broomstick, didn’t you?

vocabulary.definition: (past tense) Caused a strong impression or hit.
vocabulary.example:
”

This struck them all as an excellent idea.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is unclear, indefinite, or not explicitly stated.
vocabulary.example:
”

Chrestomanci was staring vaguely up into the beech trees and did not seem to hear.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that involves physical strength, effort, or energy.
vocabulary.example: They agreed vigorously.
